title: Nāṭyaśāstra — Five Sandhis
author: Bharata Muni (attributed)
source: Nāṭyaśāstra, c. 200 BCE–200 CE. The foundational Sanskrit treatise on dramaturgy, performance, and aesthetics.

lineage.note: Independent of Aristotle and predates most Western theory. The five 'sandhis' (junctures) divide a play into segments oriented around RASA (aesthetic emotion / flavor) rather than plot conflict. The engine is the audience's emotional response, not the protagonist's struggle.

stages.name: Mukha — Opening juncture
stages.function: Establish the seed of the action; introduce the principal flavor (rasa) that will dominate.

stages.name: Pratimukha — Progression juncture
stages.function: Develop the seed; partial actions and rising involvement; obstacles emerge.

stages.name: Garbha — Development juncture (the 'womb')
stages.function: Concealment, hidden meaning, and apparent setbacks that gestate the resolution. Midpoint of psychological depth.

stages.name: Vimarśa — Reflection juncture
stages.function: Pause for consideration; the protagonist (and audience) reflect on what has happened; emotional consolidation before the close.

stages.name: Nirvahana — Conclusion juncture
stages.function: Resolution; the principal rasa is fully realized in the audience.

cross_framework_equivalence._note: Rough alignment with Western five-act structures (Horace, Freytag, Yorke) but the engine is different. Don't equate Garbha with Freytag's climax-apex even though both sit at the structural center.

applicability.limitations: Requires the editor to think in terms of dominant emotional flavors rather than plot beats. Hard to apply to films that audiences expect to follow Western conflict-engine conventions.
